topic: Pattern Matching - Match Guards with if Conditions

turns.content: Hey, I'm looking over some code you wrote for our new router module and I'm confused about how match guards work when combined with OR patterns. Specifically, does a guard condition apply to every arm in an OR group or just one of them?

That's a great observation because it trips up a lot of developers. In Rust, the match expression evaluates each arm independently for exhaustiveness checks, so if you have an OR pattern like `Some(x) | None`, the compiler still needs to ensure both branches are covered regardless of what happens inside the guard.

So that means I can't rely on a guard to make one part of an OR expression optional? Like if I write `match value { Some(x) if x > 0 => ..., None => ... }` does the compiler think `Some(x)` is still required?

Exactly. The guard doesn't change the structural exhaustiveness requirement of the pattern itself. Even though your logic might want to skip checking `Some(x)`, Rust will still require you to handle it in some way unless you explicitly merge or restructure.
I see, so if I'm trying to avoid a dead code warning by saying 'if x > 0 then ignore this arm', the compiler still flags me for missing an exhaustive match?

Yes. The guard is evaluated after the pattern matches, but it doesn't alter whether that variant was considered in the exhaustiveness analysis. You can use a guard to filter logic inside the arm, but you still need to account for all possible values of `value`.
Got it. But what if I want to capture a value in one part and then check its condition later? Like `match x { Some(n) if n > 0 => ..., None => ... }` is that allowed?
Yes, you can absolutely bind variables inside the pattern like `Some(n)` and then use them in your guard or body. The guard is where you evaluate conditions on those captured values.
So I'm writing something like this: `match Some(x) | None { Some(val) if val > 0 => println!("positive"), _ => ... }` and the compiler says it's not exhaustive?

You are right, that pattern is not exhaustive because you're missing `Some(x)` where `x <= 0`. The OR group treats `Some(val) | None` as one unit, so the guard on `Some` doesn't exempt it from being covered.
Ah, I get it now. So if I want to avoid handling negative numbers explicitly, I should probably move that logic into a separate function or use a different structure?
You can absolutely refactor it. One common pattern is to extract the conditional check into a helper function and call it from each arm, keeping your match exhaustive over the types themselves.
That makes sense. So my final code will look like `match value { Some(_) => process_positive(), None => ... }` where `process_positive()` handles the `x > 0` logic internally?
Yes, that's a clean approach. You keep your match exhaustive over the actual values (`Some(_) | None`) and let functions handle the business logic like checking if `val > 0`.
Perfect. So I was worried about dead code warnings because of how guards interact with OR patterns, but separating the condition into a function solves it?
Exactly. That's a solid refactor for your router module. It keeps your match exhaustive while letting you handle conditional logic cleanly without confusing the compiler.
